Article in Education Week (June 14, 1995) documenting parent-school controversy in Cranston, Rhode Island, over 'descriptive' report cards.
Scientific analysis of student achievement under pass-fail grading versus traditional letter grading at Knox College and other institutions.
evidence.source_reference: Gold, 'Academic Achievement Declines Under Pass-Fail Grading,' 1971; Quann, 'Pass/Fail Grading—An Unsuccess Story,' 1974

evidence.description: Research on pass/fail grading modes in colleges during the 1960s and 70s showing graded students learn more.
UCLA CRESST newsletter quoting an expert on 'substantive equity' and 're-educating' the public.
Paul Diederich's study of 300 student papers graded by 53 different readers, showing massive grading variance.
evidence.source_reference: Diederich, French, and Carlton, 'Factors in Judgments of Writing Ability' (1961)

evidence.description: Paul Diederich study showing 300 papers graded by 53 graders resulted in 1/3 of papers receiving every possible grade from A to D.
evidence.source_reference: Diederich, French, and Carlton, 'Factors in Judgments of Writing Ability' (1961)
evidence.source_passage: When 300 student papers were graded by fifty-three graders (a total of 15,900 readings), more than one third of the papers received every possible grade.

evidence.description: ETS internal findings on the instability of grader agreement and the necessity of constant recalibration by 'table leaders.'
evidence.source_passage: During each ETS reading session, the various ‘table leaders’ must confer with each other continually to keep the groups in tune.

evidence.description: College Board research finding that essay grades depended more on the year or reader than the student's writing, prompting the shift to multiple-choice.
evidence.source_reference: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman, 'The Measurement of Writing Ability'
evidence.source_passage: one of the College Board’s reasons for instituting multiple-choice testing was the finding that the high-stakes grades given to a student’s performance-based test ‘might well depend more on which year he appeared for the examination, or on which person read his paper...’

evidence.description: Sondra Perl's study showing that even objective measures like spelling and punctuation are higher when the topic is familiar.
evidence.source_reference: Perl, 'The Composing Processes of Unskilled College Writers'
evidence.source_passage: Sondra Perl and her colleagues at the City University of New York have shown that even on a measure as objective as spelling and punctuation, quality of performance is higher when the topic is more familiar to the writer.

evidence.description: Daniel Koretz's analysis of Vermont's portfolio assessment program showing fatal unreliability in scoring.
evidence.source_reference: Koretz et al., 'The Vermont Portfolio Assessment Program' (1994)
evidence.source_passage: In Vermont, a recent large-scale attempt to use performance-based tests was analyzed by Daniel Koretz... 'The unreliability of scoring alone was sufficient to preclude most of the intended uses of the scores.'

evidence.description: Daniel Koretz's 1994 analysis of Vermont's portfolio-based testing, showing low inter-rater agreement and failure to discern quality differences.
evidence.source_passage: The unreliability of scoring alone was sufficient to preclude most of the intended uses of the scores. ... The rate of exact agreement was 45%, but the rate expected by chance alone was 35%.

evidence.description: A study of 1,300 students across 15 states comparing five essays scored by five different graders with multiple-choice tests.
evidence.source_passage: Data for the study were obtained from 1,300 eleventh- and twelfth-grade students ... Each student completed five different types of essays on required topics ... and also took several batteries of multiple-choice tests.

evidence.description: Correlation data showing that multiple-choice sections correlated at .775 with student writing ability, compared to .640 for essays read three times.
evidence.source_passage: The best correlation with the prior criterion (that is, with twenty-five readings of five papers) is .775—an acceptable level of fairness.

evidence.description: A validity study showing that a combination of multiple-choice and writing samples achieved a .784 coefficient against the criterion, compared to .775 for the objective test alone.
evidence.source_reference: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman, The Measurement of Writing Ability (1966)
evidence.source_passage: Godshalk and his colleagues determined that an hour test consisting of two multiple-choice segments plus a writing sample yielded a still higher test validity against the criterion—that is, .784 vs. .775.

evidence.description: A graph illustrating the relationship between the number of test items and test reliability, showing diminishing returns after approximately 80 items.
evidence.source_passage: Adding further items typically improves reliability only very gradually, as the following graph from Ebel illustrates.

evidence.description: Examples of three types of multiple-choice items (Usage, Sentence Correction, Construction) that were proven to be highly informative in sampling writing ability.
evidence.source_reference: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman, The Measurement of Writing Ability
evidence.source_passage: In that study, researchers experimented with six different types of multiple-choice items. It will be instructive to look at three types of items that proved to be highly informative in sampling a student’s writing ability.

evidence.description: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman's data showing objective English questions effectively discriminate ability levels.
evidence.source_reference: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman, The Measurement of Writing Ability (1966)
evidence.source_passage: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman, after examining all of their item types in detail, were quite justified in concluding the following... No convincing support for this hypothesis was found.

evidence.description: Sophisticated test theory developed by Cronbach and Messick requiring justification for correlations.
evidence.source_passage: More recently, however, thanks to the work of scientists like Cronbach and Messick, test theory has become more sophisticated and demanding.

evidence.description: A mathematical problem regarding the Earth's radius and circumference used to demonstrate higher-order thinking in objective formats.
evidence.source_passage: If the radius of the earth were increased by 3 feet, its circumference at the equator would be increased by about how much?

evidence.description: California's drop to the bottom in national reading scores following the adoption of matrix testing and naturalistic reading policies.
Research indicating that all non-impaired children can reach reading competence by the end of first grade.
Anecdote of a school superintendent who used the same multiple-choice test year after year, facilitating abuse.
An experiment where students in an urban district were given an unfamiliar standardized test alongside their usual familiar one, resulting in significantly lower scores on the unfamiliar test.
evidence.source_reference: Koretz et al., 'The Effects of High-Stakes Testing on Achievement: Preliminary Findings About Generalization Across Tests'
evidence.source_passage: He simply gave to a group of students in an urban public school district an additional set of standardized tests, which happened to be unfamiliar ones, not currently used in the district... the children performed much worse on the unexpected test than on the familiar one.

evidence.description: The administrative practice of creating entirely new questions for each administration to prevent cheating.
evidence.source_passage: Providers of the SAT and some other tests ensure this cheating-proof system by creating new questions for each administration of the test.

evidence.description: Discovery that elementary achievement scores in every state were 'above average' due to misleading reporting and test misuse.
evidence.source_reference: Cannell, 'Nationally Normed Elementary Achievement Testing in America’s Public Schools'
evidence.source_passage: Recently, the press gave a lot of attention to the “Lake Wobegon effect,” discovered by Dr. John J. Cannell, which made the scores in every state “above average.”

evidence.description: The 'Lake Wobegon effect' discovery where test reporting made every state appear above average.
evidence.source_reference: Cannell, 'Nationally Normed Elementary Achievement Testing in America's Public Schools'
evidence.source_passage: Recently, the press gave a lot of attention to the “Lake Wobegon effect,” discovered by Dr. John J. Cannell, which made the scores in every state “above average.”

evidence.description: The Chicago school system's implementation of eight parallel forms of standardized tests used annually.
evidence.source_passage: Currently, eight parallel forms of standard tests are available for use in the city’s school system each year, and no one knows until the last minute which form will be chosen.

evidence.description: A summary of the prosecution's case against multiple-choice tests citing five specific damages to teaching and learning.
evidence.source_passage: America’s obsession with multiple-choice tests has damaged teaching and learning by... putting too much value on recall and rote learning... promoting the misleading impression that there is a single right answer...

evidence.description: A 1984 article by psychometrician Norman Frederiksen summarizing research on the shortcomings and capabilities of multiple-choice formats.
evidence.source_passage: The research on the shortcomings of the multiple-choice format was summarized by the psychometrician Norman Frederiksen in a much-cited article of 1984.

evidence.description: A review article by Swanson and colleagues analyzing decades of performance-based assessment in the medical profession.
evidence.source_reference: Swanson, Norman, and Linn, "Performance Based Assessment," 1995
evidence.source_passage: Swanson and others, which showed that in the area of study where real-world higher-order skills have been most consistently tested—medicine—the decades-long attempt to produce fair and accurate performance tests of productive skills has ended in failure.

evidence.description: Frederiksen's research report stating that format factors do not influence what a test measures, even in mathematical reasoning.
A hypothetical multiple-choice question about eliminating smog in La Gomma, CA, used to demonstrate that MC questions can require complex evaluation of multiple variables.
Math tests that provide space for students to write down all computations, adding a literal performance component to a multiple-choice structure.
An apple distribution math problem requiring pattern recognition and division shortcuts rather than simple counting.
A geometry problem involving four distinct lines that requires the production of a mental or physical figure to solve.
National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) data showing a decline in higher-order thinking skills while basic skills slightly improved.
The success of the 1970s effort by states to ensure basic skills acquisition through testing.
Barbara Lerner's argument that standardized tests can effectively measure higher-order skills.
The American Psychological Association's (APA) definition of test bias as differential validity relative to a real-world criterion.
evidence.source_reference: American Psychological Association, Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing

evidence.description: The College Board's recent 'renorming' of the SAT, which raised the national mean through a formulaic adjustment.
Linda Gottfredson's analysis regarding the scientific and political problems with selective race-norming.
evidence.source_reference: Gottfredson, 'The Science and Politics of Race-Norming'

evidence.description: Analysis showing the wage gap between races closes when accounting for actual educational achievement (AFQT scores).
evidence.source_passage: Instead, Ferguson studied black-white wage inequality in terms of actual educational achievement. Once he made that adjustment, he found that the wage gap between the races closed dramatically.

evidence.description: Studies showing AFQT scores reliably predict job performance in the military.
evidence.source_passage: scores on the AFQT have been shown to reliably predict the quality of job performance in the military services.

evidence.description: Success and oversubscription of parent-run schools offering mainstream academic training in minority communities.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (direct observation in Bronx, Baltimore, Fort Collins)
evidence.source_passage: Wherever public schools have offered the choice of truly effective mainstream academic training in reading, writing, and mathematics, minority families have signed up in disproportionate numbers.

evidence.description: The historical development of Koine Greek, Latin, and English as marketplace hybrids (lingua francas).
A parent-run school in Fort Collins, Colorado, that is oversubscribed by minority families seeking academic content.
Passage from Monty Neill's essay representing the Romantic multiculturalist view that schools should adapt to students' home cultures.
Psychometric analysis showing the SAT verbal section correlates heavily with vocabulary knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Carroll, “Psychometric Approaches to the Study of Language Abilities,” 29

evidence.description: Comparative studies demonstrating that the U.S. educational system provides less equal opportunity than other developed nations.
evidence.source_reference: Torsten Husen, Talent, Equality, and Meritocracy

evidence.description: The historical account of Justice William O. Douglas's contradictory stance on the LSAT and meritocracy in the DeFunis case.
Statistical validation data showing the LSAT accurately predicts law school performance across racial lines.
A study comparing math achievement test score curves in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States, showing bimodal (Taiwan), normal-high (Japan), and low-fairness (US) distributions.
evidence.source_passage: But in a recent study of math achievement in three nations—Taiwan, Japan, and the United States—Harold Stevenson and his colleagues produced the following three curves representing the level and distribution of test scores for students of the three countries.

evidence.description: Studies by John Bishop on the importance of high-stakes tests in motivating student effort in various countries.
evidence.source_reference: Bishop, John. 'Why U.S. Students Need Incentives to Learn' (1991) and others.
evidence.source_passage: John Bishop has shown in great detail the importance of high-stakes tests in motivating students to work hard.

evidence.description: The performance and structure of educational systems in Asia and Europe compared to the United States.
evidence.source_passage: It is a sad historical irony that... their ideal is being realized better in Asia and Europe than in the United States.

concepts.author_definition: Standardized tests, often including multiple-choice formats, used to monitor progress, provide feedback, and ensure social equity through impartial measurement.
concepts.common_alternative: Biased, trivial measurements that encourage mindlessness and belong to a 'factory model' of education.
concepts.source_passage: Without effective monitoring, neither good teaching nor good educational administration is possible. Finally, and above all, objective tests are needed for academic fairness and social equity

concepts.author_definition: A policy alternative to standardized testing that requires students to construct answers or perform tasks rather than select from alternatives; touted by reformers as measuring 'higher-order skills.'
concepts.source_passage: The most widely touted policy alternative is a national system of examinations which would employ “authentic” assessment techniques... [which] require students to construct answers, perform, or produce something for evaluation.

concepts.author_definition: A pejorative characterization used by progressive reformers to describe traditional, standardized educational practices that they believe treat children like products on an assembly line.
concepts.source_passage: Then, as now, tests were repudiated for belonging to a “factory model” of education, for introducing competition where it does not belong, for denying the individuality of students’ talents and interests

concepts.author_definition: Any test (objective or performance-based) that yields the same score for the same performance, regardless of who is scoring it; focuses on inter-rater reliability.
concepts.common_alternative: Used promiscuously to refer to multiple-choice, norm-referenced, or criterion-referenced tests.
concepts.source_passage: I shall use the term “standardized test” to mean any objective test, or combination of objective and performance tests, that yields the same score for the same performance, no matter who is doing the scoring.

concepts.author_definition: A metaphor for attacking the assessment tools (tests) because they reveal the failure of the educational system, rather than addressing the failure itself.
A progressive term for testing that attempts to be 'lifelike' and avoids standardized, numerical measures, which Hirsch characterizes as an evasion of objective standards.
A modern progressive replacement for letter grades that uses descriptive summaries, which Hirsch views as a tool to avoid clear accountability and comparison.
Motivators such as grades or testing; progressives claim they lead to superficial learning, while Hirsch argues they are essential for effective study and achievement.
An absolute version of equality that rejects any ranking or distinction that results in students receiving less than 'super grades,' fearing for their self-concept.
A meritocratic system that provides equal foundational access to all students but uses rigorous testing to select the most capable for advanced education.
Assessments made by teachers for purely instructional purposes to monitor progress within the classroom.
Assessments with significant consequences used for student placement and evaluating teaching effectiveness.
The unavoidable inconsistency in grading performance-based assessments caused by graders' differing internal standards and the different weights they assign to various components of a task.
concepts.common_alternative: Often ignored by advocates who believe rubric training can create objective fairness.
concepts.source_passage: an inexpungible arbitrariness lies at the heart of grading performance-based assessments.

concepts.author_definition: The phenomenon where a performer's quality of work changes significantly depending on the specific nature and subject matter of the task.
concepts.common_alternative: Often mistakenly viewed as a reflection of general 'writing' or 'problem-solving' skill.
concepts.source_passage: The more important performer inconsistency lies in task variability: some pianists play Liszt a lot better than they do Mozart.

concepts.author_definition: The individual in charge of a grading session who dictates the standard; Hirsch characterizes this role as an 'arbitrary grading deity.'
Tests requiring students to perform tasks, such as writing essays or compiling portfolios, which are then graded by human raters.
concepts.common_alternative: Often called 'authentic assessment' by progressive educators who view it as more valid than multiple-choice.
concepts.source_passage: Given these inconsistencies of both the grader and the performer, it is no surprise that a performance-based assessment... has a low likelihood of accurately assessing a student’s average ability to perform.

concepts.author_definition: The consistency and repeatability of a test score, specifically referring to the likelihood that different graders or different topics would yield the same result for a student.
The degree to which a test measures what it claims to measure, often expressed as a coefficient against a specific criterion.
The concept that a test is a representative selection of items from a broader domain of knowledge or skill; its fairness is dependent on how well it represents that whole.
The skill of refining prose, which Hirsch argues is inextricably linked to and essentially identical to high-level writing ability.
concepts.common_alternative: A lower-level skill distinct from and less important than the creative 'writing ability.'
concepts.source_passage: The quality of real-world writing is much more dependent on editing ability than on facility in initially getting words down on paper. The best writers edit in their minds before they write.

concepts.author_definition: Subjective tests (like essays) that Hirsch argues fail to sample creativity effectively due to necessary constraints on time and choice of topic.
concepts.common_alternative: Viewed by progressives as 'authentic' assessments that are superior to multiple-choice tests.
concepts.source_passage: The creative, constructive side of writing is not well sampled even in the best performance tests, since no reliable performance test can offer free choice of topics and unlimited time.

concepts.author_definition: A form of testing that takes random samples of different test sections from various students to create a systemic diagnosis without providing individual scores.
concepts.common_alternative: Standardized testing intended for individual student evaluation.
concepts.source_passage: California has been a leader in matrix testing, which takes random samples of different sections of tests from individual students and schools and puts them all together in a mathematically valid way to get a sound large-scale result.

concepts.author_definition: Accountability measures that transcend individual students and teachers, yielding no-fault results that typically only advocate for increased spending rather than individual improvement.
Test types that claim to replicate real-world activities more closely than objective tests, but which the author characterizes as uncontrolled and unfair.
Tests designed to replicate real-world reading activities, which Hirsch argues are actually uncontrolled, unfair, and fail their own goal of real-world replication.
concepts.common_alternative: Assessments that use 'real' books or tasks rather than multiple-choice questions to measure reading proficiency.
concepts.source_passage: What this argument fails to disclose, however, is that more “authentic” types of reading tests are not only uncontrolled, hence unfair, but also unable to replicate real-world reading.

concepts.author_definition: Different versions of the same test that are similar in format and difficulty and sample the same domain but contain different specific items.
concepts.common_alternative: Often simply called 'different versions' or 'Test Form A/B'.
concepts.source_passage: Every maker of tests provides these parallel forms, which are similar in format, in difficulty, and in the domains being sampled, but vary in specific items.

concepts.author_definition: A phenomenon where test scores in every state are reported as being 'above average' due to a combination of misleading reporting and narrow teaching to the test.
concepts.common_alternative: Statistical inflation of standardized test scores.
concepts.source_passage: the “Lake Wobegon effect,” discovered by Dr. John J. Cannell, which made the scores in every state “above average.”

concepts.author_definition: The practice of focusing instruction exclusively on the specific content and format of an upcoming standardized test rather than teaching the broader subject matter.
concepts.source_passage: silent pressure on teachers to teach narrowly in order to do so [keep scores inaccurately high]

concepts.author_definition: Cognitive abilities involving reasoning, problem-solving, and deep understanding, as opposed to rote recall.
concepts.common_alternative: Often used by antitesters to mean 'real-world performance' or 'creativity' which cannot be tested by standardized means.
concepts.source_passage: Standardized tests cannot distinguish between students who understand underlying concepts and students who are only able to perform procedures by rote

concepts.author_definition: The hypothesis that the medium of a test (multiple-choice vs. essay) changes the underlying cognitive trait or ability being measured.
concepts.common_alternative: The assumption that multiple-choice inherently measures 'lower' skills than open-ended formats.
concepts.source_passage: Investigators in general, and sometimes to their surprise, “have found no evidence of format factors.”

concepts.author_definition: The extent to which performance on a specific test task predicts performance on other tasks or in real-world situations.
A test that requires the integration of information, generation of ideas, and capacity to solve problems; Hirsch argues well-designed standardized tests meet this definition inherently.
concepts.common_alternative: Tests that require students to create a product or perform a task (like an essay or experiment) rather than selecting a pre-set answer.
concepts.source_passage: A well-constructed and designed standardized test of math or reading is inherently a performance test which requires the 'integration of information and the generation of ideas' and which 'engages [students’] capacities to structure tasks, produce ideas, and solve problems.'

concepts.author_definition: The active process of working out multi-step problems and evaluating alternatives using relevant background knowledge.
The practice of reusing test items and 'teaching to the test' such that students can answer via passive recall and rote memorization.
A consistent difference between the performance of a subgroup on a test and their performance on the real-world construct the test is intended to measure (differential validity).
concepts.common_alternative: Any difference in average scores between racial, ethnic, or socioeconomic groups.
concepts.source_passage: The American Psychological Association has described test bias as a consistent difference between the way a definable subgroup of test takers perform on a test and the way they perform on some real-world criterion or construct that the test is meant to measure.

concepts.author_definition: The mathematical adjustment of test scores to change the mean or eliminate gaps between groups without changing the underlying performance levels.
concepts.source_passage: One simply takes the scores from each 'racial and socio-economic' group that shows a gap, and then one makes a mathematical adjustment for all members of that group. This is called 'renorming.'

concepts.author_definition: The harm caused to students by giving them inflated scores or grades that mask their lack of real-world competency, thereby depriving them of the skills needed for economic success.
concepts.source_passage: the gravest objection to attempting to achieve educational equity through adjusting test scores is the practical injustice it commits upon those it proposes to help. Getting a high score on a test is not the ticket to social equality for students whose actual educational competencies are low

concepts.author_definition: Statistics based on years of schooling or grades attained, which the author argues are increasingly disconnected from actual knowledge due to social promotion.
An economic and social space that functions through hybrid lingua francas (like standard English) to facilitate communication between strangers, independent of their primary ethnic identities.
A hybrid, shared public culture (lingua franca) designed to enable communication between strangers for political and economic participation.
The ideal that individuals maintain a home culture while simultaneously adapting to a shared, public 'world-city' culture for common benefit.
The fragmentation of the school system and its standards into separate ethnic or cultural enclaves, which Hirsch argues is impractical and inequitable.
A definition of fairness that seeks to make test scores and outcomes the same for all social groups, often favored by 'socially progressive' educators.

A definition of fairness that focuses on ensuring all individuals have the same access to the foundational preparation needed to succeed, which the author argues is the necessary precondition for any fair system.
The assumption that academic performance is fixed by a student's social or racial background, used by the author to critique the practice of lowering standards or renorming tests for minority groups.
The body of knowledge and vocabulary acquired primarily at home by advantaged students, which enables them to overcome the deficiencies of a 'gap-ridden' school curriculum.
The process of providing explicit, shared requirements and high-quality instruction to ensure all students, regardless of background, gain the knowledge necessary for equal opportunity.
The 'delusion' that learning is natural and that academic motivation should come from within rather than through extrinsic discipline and effort.
concepts.source_passage: The Romantic idea that learning is natural, and that the motivation for academic achievement comes from within, is an illusion that forms one of the greatest barriers to social justice imaginable

concepts.author_definition: The view that human beings require external incentives, discipline, and effort to overcome natural tendencies and achieve learning.
A hybrid language created by the marketplace to facilitate communication and commerce between strangers from different home cultures.
A Romantic concept (Blood and Soil) that posits culture is derived from the soil and intrinsic to the biological essence of a people.
A statistical distribution with two distinct peaks, used here to describe a system that tracks students into regular and elite streams.
A definition of fairness that focuses on ensuring every individual has equal access to valued goods and opportunities.
A definition of fairness that demands equal results or outcomes for all groups, often through adjusting measurement standards.

cases.name: Iowa Test Score Decline
cases.description: In the 1970s, standardized test scores declined sharply in Iowa, despite the state being 98% white and largely middle-class. This served as a counter-example to the claim that test declines were caused by cultural bias against minorities, as the decline occurred in a population the tests were allegedly biased in favor of.
key_passages: Jencks also refuted the NEA claim that standardized tests were “biased in favor of white middle-class culture” by pointing out that scores had declined sharply in Iowa, which was 98 percent white, and largely “middle class” in values and outlook.

cases.name: Cranston, Rhode Island Controversy
cases.description: A 1995 conflict between parents and school administrators over an attempt to replace letter grades with 'descriptive' report cards. Parents resisted the change, viewing it as a move away from clear standards, while administrators framed it as 'research-based.'
key_passages: In the Rhode Island city of Cranston, an intense controversy between parents and the schools had arisen over yet another attempt to introduce “descriptive” rather than “evaluative” report cards.

cases.name: Vermont Portfolio Assessment (1994)
cases.description: A large-scale attempt to replace standardized tests with portfolio-based performance assessments. Analysis by Daniel Koretz found that the scores were too unreliable for educational use and failed to differentiate student quality effectively.
key_passages: In Vermont, a recent large-scale attempt to use performance-based tests was analyzed by Daniel Koretz
The unreliability of scoring alone was sufficient to preclude most of the intended uses of the scores.

cases.name: Educational Testing Service (ETS) Essay Grading
cases.description: ETS attempts to train graders to agree on essay scores. Hirsch describes the process as requiring constant, expensive interventions to maintain a temporary and arbitrary consensus that quickly dissolves without 'recalibration.'
key_passages: the ETS has also discovered this momentarily achieved agreement to be arbitrary and unstable.
even after the lapse of just a weekend, the groups need to engage in recalibration sessions.

cases.name: The English Composition Test (ECT)
cases.description: An examination discussed in the context of the Godshalk study that combines objective multiple-choice items with an essay component to measure writing ability and signal educational priorities.
key_passages: An essay in the English Composition Test says to the student that skill in actual writing is an important outcome of instruction.

cases.name: The Godshalk Experiment
cases.description: A study that tested whether multiple-choice questions only discriminated at low skill levels while 'interlinear' exercises (context-based editing) worked for higher levels. The study found no support for the hypothesis that MC tests are superficial, concluding they effectively measure writing ability.
key_passages: One hypothesis of this study was that the multiple-choice questions were discriminating primarily at the lower levels of skill... No convincing support for this hypothesis was found.

cases.name: California's reading reform
cases.description: California implemented matrix testing, which removed individual accountability, alongside a state policy favoring 'naturalistic' reading instruction. This combination led to the state falling to the bottom of national reading score rankings.
key_passages: California has been a leader in matrix testing... the result was California’s dropping to the bottom in national reading scores.
When the two kinds of evasion are conjoined, the results can be disheartening and even tragic, as California has shown.
contestable_aspects: The causal link between matrix testing and reading score declines
The characterization of California's reading policy as solely 'naturalistic'

cases.name: The Koretz experiment
cases.description: Daniel Koretz conducted a study in an urban public school district to test the generalization of achievement. He administered unfamiliar standardized tests to students who were accustomed to a different set of tests. Even though the two tests were highly correlated theoretically, students scored significantly lower on the unfamiliar test, proving that their high scores on the familiar test were the result of narrow coaching rather than broad domain mastery.
key_passages: Not long ago, Daniel Koretz conducted a well-designed experiment that exposed the breadth of the corruption which had long been suspected.

the children performed much worse on the unexpected test than on the familiar one. This result meant that the district’s usual test scores were significantly overstating students’ knowledge and skill—a distortion that is undoubtedly widespread.
contestable_aspects: The assumption that 'narrow instruction' was the *only* cause of the score difference.
The degree to which this 'corruption' is truly 'widespread' vs. specific to certain high-stakes environments.

cases.name: Chicago school system
cases.description: Chicago implemented a system using eight parallel forms of standard tests. This was done to increase the probity of testing by ensuring that neither teachers nor students knew which version of the test would be administered until the last minute.
key_passages: Currently, eight parallel forms of standard tests are available for use in the city’s school system each year, and no one knows until the last minute which form will be chosen. The immediate effect of this practice on teaching and scores remains to be seen, but it self-evidently represents an advance in probity.

cases.name: Medical Profession Performance Testing
cases.description: The medical field attempted for decades to replace or supplement multiple-choice tests with situational, performance-based assessments to measure real-world skills. Research reviews (Swanson et al.) found these efforts failed because expertise proved context-specific, scoring was unreliable, and simulations did not evoke real-world behavior.
key_passages: in the area of study where real-world higher-order skills have been most consistently tested—medicine—the decades-long attempt to produce fair and accurate performance tests of productive skills has ended in failure.
scoring is highly inconsistent; that “generalizability is low”; that “expertise is not a general trait”; that simulations are not the real world
contestable_aspects: Whether medical board exams are representative of all types of performance assessment.
Whether the failure was due to test design or the inherent nature of the skills.

cases.name: The La Gomma Smog Question
cases.description: A thought experiment involving a small town suburb of Los Angeles considering five different plans to eliminate smog. Hirsch uses it to show that to select the correct 'None of the above' answer, a student must analyze the efficacy, practicality, and scope of various proposals using background knowledge about smog and geography.
key_passages: In 1995, the small town of La Gomma, California, a suburb of Los Angeles, considered means of eliminating smog... Which would be likely to succeed?
(E) None of the above. This is the correct answer, but it can only be known with confidence if the student thinks productively and critically about the proposed alternatives

cases.name: The Basic-Skills Movement (post-1970)
cases.description: Following a decline in basic skills, US states implemented measures and standardized tests to ensure acquisition of these skills. While successful in improving basic scores, the focus remained on lower-level skills without providing incentives for higher-order thinking, leading to a neglect of the latter.
key_passages: It is true that in the 1970s the decline in basic skills alarmed the states, which then took measures to ensure that these skills were acquired by a greater percentage of students. That effort was successful. But so long as tests remained focused on lower skills, and so long as the states provided no incentives for teaching higher ones, the practical effect of the basic-skills movement was indeed to neglect higher-order skills.

cases.name: The AFQT (Armed Forces Qualification Test)
cases.description: A multiple-choice test used by the military to measure reading, general knowledge, and math. Hirsch uses it as a prime example of a standardized test that accurately predicts real-world job performance and serves as a valid measure of 'achieved competency.'
key_passages: The AFQT is probably the best single refutation of that claim, since scores on the AFQT have been shown to reliably predict the quality of job performance in the military services.

cases.name: Fort Collins, Colorado parent-run school
cases.description: A specific instance where a school offering content-rich, mainstream training was highly sought after by minority families.
key_passages: A parent-run school of this type in Fort Collins, Colorado, is proportionately greatly oversubscribed by minority families.

cases.name: Justice Douglas and the DeFunis Case
cases.description: Supreme Court Justice William O. Douglas faced a dilemma when a white student (DeFunis) was excluded from law school despite higher scores than admitted Black students. Douglas, a believer in meritocracy, attacked the LSAT as biased without evidence to resolve the tension between his belief in talent-based hierarchy and his desire for social justice outcomes.
key_passages: Douglas decided to declare the L.S.A.T. biased although he had no evidence that it was is mainly a demonstration that he was intellectually trapped and couldn’t find any other way out.
He couldn’t reconcile his passionate belief in meritocracy with the actual meritocracy’s mechanical feeling and its tendency to reward some cultures far more than others.

cases.name: SAT Renorming
cases.description: The College Board adjusted SAT scores (renorming) and justified it by pointing to the increased diversity of the test-taking pool, which the author views as an admission of defeat regarding minority student achievement.
key_passages: The College Board unwittingly expressed such hopelessness when it justified its renorming of SAT scores with the explanation that many minorities were now taking the test.

cases.name: Taiwan's Educational System
cases.description: Described as a 'meritocratic' system with a bimodal achievement curve representing a fusion of regular and elite tracks. Both tracks are coherent, driven by specific yearly requirements, and monitored, resulting in average scores much higher than the U.S.
key_passages: Taiwan’s curve is meritocratic... The meritocratic curve is bimodal, with two distinct bumps in the middle, representing a fusion of test results from the regular track and the elite track of schooling. But since both the regular and the elite tracks are coherent systems... the average scores from both the Taiwanese tracks are much higher than the U.S. average.

cases.name: Japan's Educational System
cases.description: Described as an 'egalitarian' system where all students follow the same track and receive the same high-quality curriculum and instruction. This results in a normal-shaped curve with a very high average level of competency.
key_passages: The Japanese curve, in contrast to the Taiwanese, is more egalitarian; everybody is on the same track and receives the same high-quality curriculum and instruction. The result is a neat, normal-shaped curve, but one that displays a very high average level of competency.

cases.name: Excellent national systems (Asia and Europe)
cases.description: Educational systems that employ explicit requirements and student-led instructional tasks to bring all children to a high level of achievement, effectively realizing the Jeffersonian common school ideal.
key_passages: In such systems, all children are brought up to the required high level, enabling the class as a whole to move forward step by step.
It is a sad historical irony that... their ideal is being realized better in Asia and Europe than in the United States.

thinkers.name: George Madaus
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized tests are under heavy attack from reformers who propose 'authentic assessment' as a way to improve academic performance for all subgroups.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Madaus's compilation of sources to define the scope and nature of the 'tumult' in American testing and the specific claims made by anti-test reformers.
key_passages: George Madaus, himself no slouch as an attacker, has excellently summarized the current scene in the following assertions, which he took from thirty-six printed sources published between 1990 and 1992

thinkers.name: Chester Finn
thinkers.key_idea: A defense of objective testing as a means for school improvement and accountability.
thinkers.source_work: Finn, We Must Take Charge: Our Schools and Our Future
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Finn's work as a vigorous defense of objective testing that aligns with his own purposes.
key_passages: A vigorous defense of objective testing having in view many of the same purposes as this chapter was presented in Finn, We Must Take Charge: Our Schools and Our Future, Chapter 10.

thinkers.name: Christopher Jencks
thinkers.key_idea: The academic decline of the 1970s was due to schools lowering standards and failing to require demanding work.
thinkers.source_work: What’s Behind the Drop in Test Scores? (1978)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Jencks to provide a historical and logical rebuttal to the NEA's attacks on testing, specifically regarding cultural bias and the measurement of higher-order skills.
key_passages: Christopher Jencks published a shrewd analysis of the reasons for the academic decline—principally, he thought, because schools no longer taught and required demanding academic work.

thinkers.name: William Heard Kilpatrick
thinkers.key_idea: Abolishing tests and grades is a necessary step in a progressive education system that has discarded a subject-matter curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Kilpatrick as the primary example of the 'progressive-Romantic' origin of anti-testing sentiment in American education.
key_passages: When William Heard Kilpatrick designed his demonstration school at Teachers College, one of his first innovations, after having dispensed with the subject-matter curriculum, was to abolish tests and grades.

thinkers.name: William Blake
thinkers.key_idea: An abhorrence of connecting human beings with numerical measurement ('number, weight & measure').
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Blake's poetry to illustrate the deep-seated Romantic roots of the anti-testing movement.
key_passages: As Blake put it with customary trenchancy: “Bring out number, weight & measure in a year of dearth.”

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: A meritocratic education system providing equal foundations but rigorous selection via testing.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch contrasts Jefferson's 'meritocratic equality' with modern 'Romantic egalitarianism' to show that testing is compatible with democracy.
key_passages: Jeffersonian democratic egalitarianism, which aimed to give rich and poor the same foundations for achievement, but to be quite rigorous in selecting only the better students

thinkers.name: Paul Diederich
thinkers.key_idea: Subjectivity in essay grading makes performance-based assessment unreliable.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Diederich's 1961 study to prove that subjective grading of writing leads to massive inconsistency and lack of fairness.
key_passages: In 1961, Paul Diederich and his colleagues proved that this student belief is no myth.

thinkers.name: Sondra Perl
thinkers.key_idea: Writing performance (including mechanics) is higher when the topic is familiar.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Perl to support his claim that background knowledge/familiarity is the primary driver of performance, even in supposedly 'neutral' skills like spelling.
key_passages: Sondra Perl and her colleagues... have shown that even on a measure as objective as spelling and punctuation, quality of performance is higher when the topic is more familiar to the writer.

thinkers.name: Daniel Koretz
thinkers.key_idea: Portfolio assessment systems in practice suffer from fatal unreliability in scoring.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Koretz's analysis of the Vermont program as the empirical 'last word' on why large-scale performance testing fails.
key_passages: In Vermont, a recent large-scale attempt to use performance-based tests was analyzed by Daniel Koretz, who reached the following conclusions...

thinkers.name: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman
thinkers.key_idea: Multiple-choice tests correlate more highly with actual writing ability than human-graded essays unless the essays are graded dozens of times.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch relies on their statistical analysis to argue for the efficiency and fairness of objective testing over subjective essay grading.
key_passages: Godshalk and his colleagues determined that an hour test consisting of two multiple-choice segments plus a writing sample yielded a still higher test validity against the criterion

thinkers.name: Winston Churchill
thinkers.key_idea: Democracy is the worst form of government except for all those other forms that have been tried.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adapts Churchill's famous defense of democracy to justify the use of standardized objective tests in high-stakes settings.
key_passages: The reason the psychometric community defends their use for high-stakes, summative tests is that, bad as objective tests might seem to be, they are, as Churchill said of democracy, better than all the other systems.

thinkers.name: Lee Cronbach
thinkers.key_idea: The need for 'test validation' and a sophisticated theory to justify why test scores correlate with real-world criteria.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Cronbach's work to acknowledge that modern testing must move beyond simple correlation to theoretical justification.
key_passages: More recently, however, thanks to the work of scientists like Cronbach and Messick, test theory has become more sophisticated and demanding.

thinkers.name: Samuel Messick
thinkers.key_idea: Validity in testing refers to the degree to which evidence supports the inferences made from scores.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Messick alongside Cronbach to establish the higher standards of modern psychometrics.
key_passages: Test makers are being asked to explain and justify the objective correlations between tests and the real world with an adequate and coherent theory of why the correlations exist.

thinkers.name: Connie Juel
thinkers.key_idea: Nearly every child without an organic impairment can reach reading competence by the end of the first grade.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses this researcher's claim to establish a moral and professional baseline for what teachers should be expected to achieve through proper guidance and accountability.
key_passages: I am told by researchers in the area of beginning reading that every child who is not organically impaired can be brought to an appropriate degree of reading competence by the end of first grade.

thinkers.name: Deborah Meier
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized reading tests do not actually measure the act of reading.
thinkers.source_work: Why Reading Tests Don't Test Reading
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges the criticism but dismisses it as a misleading way of saying tests are imperfect, arguing that even imperfect tests correlate well with real-world ability.
key_passages: But even this highly responsible use of standardized reading tests has been criticized on the grounds that such tests do not really test reading—a misleading way of saying that some tests are badly constructed

thinkers.name: John J. Cannell
thinkers.key_idea: The discovery that all fifty states were reporting above-average test scores.
thinkers.source_work: Nationally Normed Elementary Achievement Testing in America’s Public Schools
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Cannell to show that the public is partially aware of test inflation, though he argues the public and press miss the deeper 'process corruption' Cannell uncovered.
key_passages: Recently, the press gave a lot of attention to the “Lake Wobegon effect,” discovered by Dr. John J. Cannell, which made the scores in every state “above average.”

thinkers.name: Linda Darling-Hammond
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized tests fail to measure higher-order skills and do not support real-world task capacities.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Darling-Hammond and others as representatives of the 'antitest movement' whose charges he intends to refute as half-truths.
key_passages: Current research on human learning and performance has suggested that many currently used tests fail to measure students higher order cognitive abilities or to support their capacities to perform real world tasks.

thinkers.name: Norman Frederiksen
thinkers.key_idea: Distinguished between the expediency of multiple-choice (testing information) and its necessity (it doesn't have to be limited to rote recall, though it can't test creativity).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Frederiksen as a credible source (often cited by critics) to show that even this researcher rejected the idea that the format itself determines what is measured.
key_passages: He was careful to acknowledge that expediency was not the same as necessity, and conceded that the format itself did not require passivity and mere recall on the part of test takers.
The correlations are generally interpreted to mean that the format does not influence what a test measures.

thinkers.name: Neill and Medina
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized tests emphasize quick recognition of facts rather than integration of ideas, placing students in a passive role.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes them to establish the 'allegation' he intends to refute through his smog example and the logic of reading/math tests.
key_passages: Multiple-Choice Tests Encourage Passivity and Rote Learning... [they] exclude a great many kinds of knowledge and types of performance we expect from students, placing test takers in a passive, reactive role

thinkers.name: Philip Sidney
thinkers.key_idea: The abuse of a tool does not negate the validity of its proper use.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Sidney's defense of poetry to argue that the 'corrupt use' of multiple-choice tests (teaching to the test) does not mean the test format itself is invalid.
key_passages: But, as Sidney said in his eloquent defense of poetry against the charge that it corrupts morals, “What! Shall the abuse of a thing make the right use odious?”

thinkers.name: Barbara Lerner
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized tests can be used to drive improvements in higher-order skills just as they did for lower-order skills.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Lerner to support the inference that the format of tests does not prevent them from testing higher skills.
key_passages: the suggestion that the format of the tests prevents them from testing higher skills, whereas they could in fact help achieve the same sort of improvement in higher skills as they have accomplished in lower ones—an inference that has been eloquently argued by Barbara Lerner.

thinkers.name: Nettles and Bernstein
thinkers.key_idea: Educational equity should be pursued by eliminating racial and socioeconomic gaps on whatever measures are used to assess performance.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes them to illustrate the 'impractical' and purely ideological approach to equity that focuses on measurement adjustments rather than competency improvement.
key_passages: the dual issues of improving the ways student performance is measured, and eliminating the racial and socioeconomic gaps on whatever measures are used are enduring ones for policy makers and educators to address

thinkers.name: Linda Gottfredson
thinkers.key_idea: Selective renorming of test scores for particular groups is scientifically and politically problematic.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses her analysis to support the claim that mathematical adjustments for specific groups are widely and rightly seen as unacceptable.
key_passages: according to some solid analysis by Linda Gottfredson, should not accept the practice of renorming selectively

thinkers.name: Ronald Ferguson
thinkers.key_idea: Black-white wage inequality is primarily a function of differences in actual educational achievement/competency rather than race alone.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Ferguson's economic data to argue that the 'gatekeeping' theory of tests is wrong and that tests measure the very competencies required for economic success.
key_passages: Once he made that adjustment, he found that the wage gap between the races closed dramatically.
Ferguson’s work is thus yet another refutation of the claim that multiple-choice tests do not probe real-world competencies.

thinkers.name: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
thinkers.key_idea: The Romantic concept of 'Blut und Boden' (blood and soil) and the belief that home culture/language is the unique essence of a person.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Fichte as a source of the 'mystical concept' that cultural identity is an essence rather than an accident.
key_passages: This cosmopolitanism was regarded by Fichte and other Romantics as an effete, Frenchified concept. No one but a true German, Fichte said, could properly understand and speak German.

thinkers.name: Monty Neill
thinkers.key_idea: Advocated for 'reconstructing schooling to adapt to the students and their cultures' to resist 'monocultural' assimilation.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Neill to exemplify the 'Romantic' version of multiculturalism that he believes is impractical for achieving equity.
key_passages: The second [Romantic] approach favored by all these authors focuses on reconstructing schooling to adapt to the students and their cultures.

thinkers.name: William O. Douglas
thinkers.key_idea: The Constitution presupposes an aristocracy of talent, but tests that produce unequal racial outcomes must be biased and should be abolished.
thinkers.engagement: The author uses Douglas as an example of 'test evasion' where a lack of evidence for bias is ignored to serve an ideological need to reconcile meritocracy with unequal results.
key_passages: Douglas was forced into this position by the fact that he was a fierce believer in meritocracy... But the LSAT was statistically accurate in predicting the grades that both blacks and whites would make in law school.

thinkers.name: Nicholas Lemann
thinkers.key_idea: Douglas's attack on the LSAT was an act of intellectual desperation caused by the conflict between his meritocratic ideals and social realities.
thinkers.engagement: The author quotes Lemann extensively to provide a psychological and historical analysis of why individuals attack objective testing.
key_passages: The journalist and historian of civil rights Nicholas Lemann reports the following incident... That Douglas decided to declare the L.S.A.T. biased although he had no evidence that it was is mainly a demonstration that he was intellectually trapped.

thinkers.name: John Bishop
thinkers.key_idea: High-stakes tests are crucial for student motivation.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Bishop as the empirical authority for the claim that testing drives student effort.
key_passages: John Bishop has shown in great detail the importance of high-stakes tests in motivating students to work hard.

thinkers.name: Antonio Gramsci
thinkers.key_idea: Education requires discipline and toil; the idea of 'natural' learning is a disservice to the disadvantaged.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Gramsci as a 'wise spokesman for the disadvantaged' to validate his critique of Romantic educational theories.
key_passages: It was Antonio Gramsci... who wrote that the gravest disservice to social justice entailed by Romantic theories of education is the delusion that educational achievement comes as naturally as leaves to a tree

actors.name: Romantic progressives
actors.context: The group of educational reformers since the 1920s who have campaigned against student testing.

actors.name: George Madaus
actors.context: A scholar who summarized the current attacks on standardized testing.

actors.name: National Education Association (NEA)
actors.context: The primary institutional voice attacking standardized tests as biased and outmoded since the 1970s.

actors.name: George Madaus
actors.context: A scholar who analyzed equity issues and performance-based assessment.

actors.name: Parents of Cranston, Rhode Island
actors.context: Citizens resisting the abolition of letter grades in favor of less precise 'narrative' reports.

actors.name: William Heard Kilpatrick
actors.context: Early 20th-century educator whose anti-testing ideas define current orthodoxy.

actors.name: UCLA Center for Research on Evaluation, Standards and Student Testing (CRESST)
actors.context: Cited as an example of the testing community's shift toward favoring 'substantive equity' over merit.

actors.name: Education Week
actors.context: Source of an article quoting experts who claim grades are detrimental to students.

actors.name: Educational Testing Service (ETS)
actors.context: The organization cited for its exhaustive but ultimately failing efforts to make performance grading consistent and fair.

actors.name: College Board
actors.context: The body that moved to multiple-choice testing specifically because of the unfairness and inconsistency of essay-based performance tests.

actors.name: Daniel Koretz
actors.context: Educational researcher whose study on Vermont portfolios is used to critique performance-based testing.

actors.name: Godshalk, Swineford, and Coffman
actors.context: Researchers who conducted the definitive study comparing essay and multiple-choice testing validities.

actors.name: Antitesters
actors.context: Critics who exploit the 'repellent features' of multiple-choice tests to advocate for their total abandonment.

actors.name: Psychometric community
actors.context: The scientific community that defends objective tests as the most reliable means for high-stakes assessment.

actors.name: Psychometric community
actors.context: The professional community that defends objective tests for high-stakes use.

actors.name: Antitesters
actors.context: Critics who exploit the repellent features of standardized tests to argue against their use.

actors.name: Legislators
actors.context: Policymakers who use test scores as blunt instruments for reform without setting clear goals.

actors.name: School Superintendent
actors.context: An unnamed official who admitted through silence to the vulnerability of using the same test every year.

actors.name: Antitesters
actors.context: Groups who use the pedagogical abuses of tests as a reason to avoid all forms of accountability.

actors.name: Daniel Koretz
actors.context: Researcher who provided empirical proof of test score inflation through his experiment with unfamiliar tests.

actors.name: John J. Cannell
actors.context: Doctor who discovered the 'Lake Wobegon effect' regarding state test scores.

actors.name: The Press
actors.context: Argued to have focused on reporting scandals while ignoring the subtle corruption of the educational process.

actors.name: Chicago school system
actors.context: Cited as a model for using parallel test forms to restore integrity to standardized testing.

actors.name: The Press
actors.context: Accused of ignoring subtle corruption in the educational process and only focusing on superficial reporting scandals.

actors.name: Antitest movement
actors.context: A group of educational critics who argue standardized tests cause educational decline and are biased.

actors.name: Norman Frederiksen
actors.context: Psychometrician whose 1984 research Hirsch uses to debunk antitest claims.

actors.name: Swanson, Norman, and Linn
actors.context: Researchers who reviewed the failure of performance assessment in medicine.

actors.name: Frederiksen
actors.context: Researcher whose findings on test format are used to debunk anti-testing claims.

actors.name: Darling-Hammond
actors.context: Critic who claims standardized tests cause student passivity.

actors.name: Neill and Medina
actors.context: Authors of a report claiming multiple-choice tests exclude important types of performance.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: The source of data showing the decline in higher-order skills and the proof that multiple-choice tests can measure such skills.

actors.name: College Board
actors.context: Source of the math and geometry problem examples used to defend multiple-choice tests.

actors.name: American Psychological Association
actors.context: Provided the technical definition of test bias used to distinguish scientific measurement from ideological claims.

actors.name: The College Board
actors.context: Engaged in national renorming of SAT scores to lift the national mean.

actors.name: Ronald Ferguson
actors.context: Economist whose work on black-white wage gaps supports the author's focus on competency.

actors.name: Linda Gottfredson
actors.context: Researcher who analyzed the practice of race-norming.

actors.name: Antitesters
actors.context: Group that argues standardized tests are culturally biased and should be adjusted or abolished.

actors.name: Minority parents in the Bronx and Baltimore
actors.context: Cited as evidence that minority families desire mainstream academic training.

actors.name: Socially progressive administrators
actors.context: Described as favoring 'equality of result' and Romantic multiculturalism in schools.

actors.name: College Board
actors.context: Critiqued for 'renorming' SAT scores and displaying 'sociological determinism' toward minority students.

actors.name: Meritocrats
actors.context: A group in the educational debate that favors tracking and talent identification.

actors.name: Egalitarians
actors.context: A group in the educational debate that opposes tracking and favors 'equality of result.'

actors.name: Harold Stevenson
actors.context: Researcher whose study on math achievement in Asia vs. the US provided the data for Hirsch's argument on fairness and curriculum.

actors.name: American public education system
actors.context: Characterized as 'mediocre', 'gap-ridden', 'repetitive', and unfair because it leaves many students behind.

actors.name: Antitesting movement
actors.context: Advocates who oppose high-stakes testing in the name of social fairness, which Hirsch argues is self-defeating.

objections_raised.objection: Objective tests encourage passivity, mindlessness, and triviality.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch acknowledges that minor abuses exist but argues that properly used tests motivate effort and provide essential feedback for learning.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests are unfair to minorities and injure social justice.
objections_raised.response: Objective tests are actually necessary for academic fairness and social equity, which is why Americans pioneered them.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests do not measure students' ability to analyze, synthesize, or apply knowledge to new phenomena.
objections_raised.response: This is refuted by looking at sample questions which clearly require those higher-order skills.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests are biased in favor of white middle-class culture.
objections_raised.response: If that were true, scores would not have declined among the white middle-class population of Iowa.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests contain complex language and obscure vocabulary that penalizes students.
objections_raised.response: This is a self-fulfilling prophecy; if schools fail to teach complex prose, students will obviously struggle to read it.

objections_raised.objection: Testing and grading are dehumanizing because they try to describe complex human beings with simple letters or numbers.
objections_raised.response: Teachers use grades not to dehumanize students, but out of a sense of responsibility to honesty, fairness, and effective teaching.

objections_raised.objection: Grades inhibit learning by making it highly competitive and setting up a 'win-lose' situation.
objections_raised.response: Scientific research on pass-fail systems shows that students actually study harder and learn more when traditional grades are used.

objections_raised.objection: Giving grades inhibits learning because it makes education a highly competitive win-lose situation that damages student self-concept.
objections_raised.source: Unnamed expert quoted in Education Week
objections_raised.response: Research shows students actually learn more when graded; the objection is based on Romantic egalitarianism rather than evidence of learning outcomes.

objections_raised.objection: Professionals can be trained to agree more closely and achieve grading consistency in performance assessments.
objections_raised.response: While agreement can be achieved through socialization, it is 'momentary,' 'arbitrary,' 'unstable,' and requires constant, expensive recalibration.

objections_raised.objection: Performance-based tests (portfolios/essays) are necessary to overcome the unfairness and inaccuracy of multiple-choice tests.
objections_raised.response: Empirical data shows that performance-based tests are significantly less reliable and less fair unless they are conducted at an impossibly high financial cost; multiple-choice tests are actually more accurate indicators of average ability.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests merely test 'editing skill' rather than 'writing skill.'
objections_raised.response: While valid for classroom teaching contexts, objective tests are superior for high-stakes summative assessment because they act as better sampling devices and provide higher reliability/fairness.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests are 'repellent' and students are tired of taking them.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch acknowledges this as a valid concern for a classroom teacher but argues it doesn't invalidate the psychometric necessity of these tests for large-scale accountability.

objections_raised.objection: Objective tests only measure editing skill, not higher-level writing skill.
objections_raised.response: Excellence in editing and writing are inextricable; best writers edit in their minds, and 'writing ability' is often dependent on editing.

objections_raised.objection: The multiple-choice format imposes superficiality and rote memorization.
objections_raised.response: This is 'judging a book by its cover.' Just as a sonnet's strict form can be epic, objective questions can probe depth through ingenuity.

objections_raised.objection: Tests debase educational achievement by fragmenting instruction and encouraging 'teaching to the test.'
objections_raised.response: This is a significant abuse, but the fault lies in the mindless application of tests and the failure to set precise goals, not in testing itself.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized reading tests do not 'really' test reading or replicate real-world reading activities.
objections_raised.response: 'Authentic' tests are also unable to replicate the real world, and even flawed standardized tests correlate strongly with actual reading abilities.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests have a baleful and corrupting influence on teaching in schools.
objections_raised.response: The corruption stems from the misuse of tests by adults (collusion and narrow prepping), not the tests themselves. Standardized tests are actually the best tool to detect such corruption.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests lead to 'teaching to the test' and narrow the curriculum.
objections_raised.response: This is a valid concern but is solved by using multiple parallel test forms so teachers must teach the subject rather than specific questions.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests measure only lower-order skills and rote recall at the expense of understanding.
objections_raised.response: This is a 'facile and inaccurate' wholesale indictment; some tests do this, but others can measure complex skills, and alternatives are currently unreliable.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests cannot distinguish between deep conceptual understanding and rote procedural performance.
objections_raised.source: Antitest critics (unnamed in this specific sentence, but likely referring to Neill/Darling-Hammond)
objections_raised.response: Research into 'format factors' shows that multiple-choice tests of mathematical reasoning and other subjects measure the same attributes as other formats; format does not determine if a test is superficial.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests don't tap 'real-world' skills or higher-order skills.
objections_raised.response: The only thing they specifically cannot test is 'creativity' (producing new hypotheses); other 'higher-order' reasoning is tested effectively, and 'real-world' performance tests have failed to be reliable or valid in practice.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests can only probe surface facts rather than deep understanding.
objections_raised.response: Research (Frederiksen) shows format does not influence what a test measures and mathematical reasoning is measured the same way regardless of format.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice formats encourage student passivity and rote learning.
objections_raised.response: Well-designed problems require active 'integration of information' and 'generation of ideas.' Passivity only occurs when tests are corrupted by reuse and teaching to specific items.

objections_raised.objection: Multiple-choice tests have caused the decline in higher-order skills because they can only measure basics.
objections_raised.response: The NAEP uses multiple-choice to measure these higher skills (proving they can be measured). The decline was caused by state policy focus on basic skills, not the test format itself.

objections_raised.objection: The format of multiple-choice tests prevents them from testing higher-order skills.
objections_raised.response: This is a mistake; the neglect of higher skills is due to a lack of incentives and test focus, not format; tests can and should be made more demanding to test these skills.

objections_raised.objection: High-stakes testing is merely a gatekeeping function that prevents members of disadvantaged groups from full participation.
objections_raised.response: This is a grave oversimplification of economic realities; Ferguson's work shows that when actual competency is equal, the wage gap largely disappears, meaning the tests measure real economic hurdles.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests are biased against certain cultural and linguistic groups.
objections_raised.response: They are 'culturally biased' only in the sense that groups score differently, but they are not 'technically biased' because they accurately predict real-world marketplace performance.

objections_raised.objection: Mainstream, 'monocultural' schooling is a form of Eurocentric assimilation that minority students resist.
objections_raised.response: Effective schooling must be 'monocultural' (a hybrid lingua franca) to enable universal participation; furthermore, the claim of 'resistance' is not borne out by the eagerness of minority parents to seek such training.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests (like the SAT or LSAT) are culturally biased against minorities.
objections_raised.response: The tests are not biased; they are statistically accurate predictors of performance. The perceived bias is actually the test reflecting the 'real-world' failure of schools to provide students with the necessary intellectual capital and preparation.

objections_raised.objection: The United States is a diverse nation with unique problems, making it impossible to achieve the same educational results or equality of opportunity as other nations.
objections_raised.source: Defenders of 'disappointments of Head Start and of our school results generally'
objections_raised.response: This is an exaggerated fact used as an excuse. Comparing different systems (like Taiwan's meritocratic and Japan's egalitarian systems) shows that high-quality, coherent curriculum achieves fairness regardless of the specific ideological approach to tracking.

objections_raised.objection: Using high-stakes tests as gatekeepers and monitors is socially unfair.
objections_raised.response: High-stakes tests are actually essential to social fairness because they provide the monitoring and incentives necessary for equity and excellence, particularly for disadvantaged students who need external motivation to overcome home-based deficits.
